article_title: Homelab projects to try this weekend (March 6 - 8)
core_thesis: This article presents three practical homelab projects for the weekend: using Tdarr to transcode media libraries for storage efficiency, deploying Portainer for simplified Docker container management, and setting up Nginx Proxy Manager for reverse proxy access to self-hosted services. The author emphasizes that these tools save time, reduce complexity, and improve homelab organization, with a bonus suggestion to explore Grafana for monitoring dashboards.
detailed_summary: The article, published on How-To Geek, targets homelab enthusiasts looking for quick, impactful projects. The first project involves Tdarr, a tool that automatically transcodes media files (e.g., from H.264 to H.265) to save storage space. The author reports saving 7TB by configuring Tdarr to only transcode files above a certain bitrate and strip unnecessary audio/subtitle tracks. The second project is Portainer, a web-based Docker management interface that replaces the Docker CLI. The author uses Portainer with agents on multiple Docker hosts to manage containers from a single UI, noting it requires just two commands to deploy. The third project is Nginx Proxy Manager, a reverse proxy tool that allows accessing self-hosted services via domain names instead of IP:port combinations. The author runs nearly 50 reverse proxy domains and provides tips on port forwarding and adjusting internal ports (e.g., using 81/444) when 80/443 are occupied. The article concludes with a recommendation to set up Grafana for a comprehensive homelab dashboard, though it requires several hours of configuration.

entities.name: Patrick Campanale
entities.role: Author and homelab enthusiast sharing personal experiences with Tdarr, Portainer, and Nginx Proxy Manager.

entities.name: Tdarr
entities.role: Media transcoding tool that optimizes video files to save storage space.

entities.name: Portainer
entities.role: Docker management UI that simplifies container and stack deployment.

entities.name: Nginx Proxy Manager
entities.role: Reverse proxy tool for managing access to self-hosted services via domain names.

entities.name: Grafana
entities.role: Monitoring and dashboarding tool recommended for visualizing homelab metrics.

key_data_points: Tdarr can transcode media from H.264 to H.265, saving significant storage; the author saved 7TB by configuring it to only process files above a certain bitrate and strip extra audio/subtitles. (🟢 Relevant to homelab storage optimization: reduces disk usage, freeing space for other projects.)

Portainer allows managing Docker containers via a web UI, supporting both individual containers and Docker Compose stacks. It can be deployed with two commands and uses agents to control multiple Docker hosts from one interface. (🟢 Useful for DevOps/homelab: simplifies container management without CLI, ideal for multi-server setups.)

Nginx Proxy Manager enables reverse proxying for self-hosted services, supporting internal and external access via domain names. The author runs nearly 50 domains through it and recommends port forwarding external 80/443 to the container. (🟢 Critical for homelab networking: eliminates need to remember IP:port combos, enhances security and accessibility.)

The author uses non-standard ports (81/444) for Nginx Proxy Manager when 80/443 are occupied, relying on router-level port forwarding (e.g., Unifi system) to map external ports. (🟢 Practical tip for homelab: demonstrates flexibility in port allocation when multiple services compete for standard ports.)

Grafana is suggested as a follow-up project for building a homelab dashboard, but requires several hours of setup. (🟢 Relevant for monitoring: provides centralized visibility into system metrics, useful for troubleshooting and capacity planning.)
methodology_steps: Tdarr setup: Deploy Tdarr in Docker, configure transcoding rules (e.g., target codec H.265, minimum bitrate threshold), and enable stripping of extra audio/subtitle tracks. Monitor progress and verify storage savings.

Portainer deployment: Run the Portainer server container with a persistent volume, then install Portainer Agent on other Docker hosts. Connect agents to the server via the UI to manage all containers from a single dashboard.

Nginx Proxy Manager installation: Deploy the Docker container, map ports (e.g., 81:81, 444:443 if 80/443 are in use), and configure port forwarding on the router to direct external 80/443 to the container's internal ports. Add proxy hosts for each service with domain names and SSL certificates.
conclusions: Tdarr is an effective tool for reclaiming storage in media servers by transcoding inefficient codecs, with the author saving 7TB. This is particularly valuable for homelabs with large Plex/Jellyfin libraries, as it automates a tedious process and can be fine-tuned to avoid re-encoding already optimized files.

Portainer significantly reduces the complexity of Docker management, especially for users who dislike the CLI. Its agent-based architecture allows centralized control across multiple hosts, making it a must-have for homelabs running numerous containers.

Nginx Proxy Manager provides a user-friendly way to expose self-hosted services securely via reverse proxies. The article's practical advice on port mapping and router configuration helps overcome common setup hurdles, enabling both internal and external access without exposing raw ports.

The article encourages homelab enthusiasts to start with these foundational tools before moving to advanced monitoring with Grafana, emphasizing that incremental improvements lead to a more organized and efficient homelab.
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly supports your homelab and DevOps interests by providing step-by-step guidance on three essential tools: Tdarr for media optimization, Portainer for Docker management, and Nginx Proxy Manager for reverse proxying. As a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast, you can leverage Portainer to streamline container deployments for development and testing environments, while Nginx Proxy Manager can be used to securely expose web applications or APIs. The storage savings from Tdarr are beneficial if you run a media server, and the Grafana suggestion aligns with monitoring needs for both homelab and potential Red Teaming infrastructure. The practical tips on port forwarding and multi-host management are immediately applicable to your homelab setup.
extra_advice: Consider combining Portainer with Nginx Proxy Manager to create a secure, centralized access point for all your self-hosted services. For Red Teaming, you could use Nginx Proxy Manager to expose phishing or C2 servers behind a reverse proxy, adding a layer of obfuscation. Additionally, explore Tdarr's plugin system to automate other media processing tasks, such as stripping metadata that could leak information.
